title: Johnson -v- The Teaching Council
text:
UNAPPROVED NO REDACTION NEEDED
THE COURT OF APPEAL
CIVIL
Neutral Citation Number: [2026] IECA 13
Court of Appeal Record Number: 2025/94
High Court Record Number: 2022/456 JR
Binchy J.
Butler J.
Meenan J.
BETWEEN/
GEARÓID JOHNSON
APPELLANT
- AND –
THE TEACHING COUNCIL
RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT of Mr. Justice Charles Meenan delivered on the 13th day of February
2026
Introduction: -
1. This is an appeal from the judgment and order of the High Court (Bolger J.) refusing
the appellant orders for discovery, replies to particulars and an order striking out the
statement of opposition filed on behalf of the respondent.

- 2 -
2. At issue in this application is the interpretation and effect of certain provisions of the
Teaching Council Act, 2001 (as amended) (the “Act of 2001”) that provide for the
consideration and investigation of complaints made against teachers. In general terms
these provisions provide procedures to decide whether a complaint meets certain criteria to
warrant further action being taken by way of a disciplinary hearing. It should be noted that
other statutes that provide for the consideration and determination of complaints against
professionals e.g., Doctors, Dentists, Solicitors and Pharmacists have similar provisions.
Background: -
3. The appellant qualified as a secondary teacher in 1996. At the time leave was
granted in these judicial review proceedings (4 July 2022) the appellant had some 26 years
of teaching experience for the most part in the Ballyfermot College of Further Education, a
constituent college of the City of Dublin Education and Training Board (“CDETB”). The
CDETB is the patron of Ballyfermot College, as provided for in the Education and
Training Boards Act, 2013. At all material times until 2020 the appellant was an employee
of CDETB. The appellant is currently working as a teacher at Moyle Park College,
Clondalkin, Dublin 12.
4. Over a number of years the appellant became active on social media posting on
issues concerning gender, race and religion. In April 2015 two individuals in the
employment of CDETB made a series of complaints to the management of Ballyfermot
College about certain postings they had seen on the appellant’s private social media
account. Subsequently, the appellant had a meeting with management and gave assurances
that no mention of the college would ever be made by him on social media. Nevertheless,
the complainants continued to complain about the appellant’s social media posts.
5. In May 2015 it would appear that a decision was taken that no further action would
be taken concerning the complaints. In October 2016 a further complaint was made by a

- 3 -
mature student which, the appellant believed, involved the complainants from 2015. This
complaint concerned an alleged opinion given in class by the appellant concerning the
“Marriage Referendum” held in 2015. A meeting followed, after which the appellant
deleted his social media profile as, what he described as being, “an act of good will”.
However, this was not the end of the matter.
6. In December 2017, the original two complainants made a formal complaint against
the appellant alleging “workplace bullying”. Investigations were commenced concerning
the appellant. There then followed a suspension in October 2018 from which a
disciplinary process ensued.
7. The result of the disciplinary process, which was protracted, was that in January
2020 the appellant was notified that his employment was to be terminated. The appellant,
unsuccessfully, appealed this decision pursuant to the provision of the Education Act,
1998.
8. In a letter dated 14 January 2020 the Chief Executive Office of the CDETB made a
complaint to the “professional standards” section of the respondent.
The Complaint: -
9. The letter of 14 January 2020 set out the details of the complaint including extracts
from some twelve social media postings made by the appellant. It was stated that these
postings “... could reasonably be deemed to impact negatively on the learning and working
environment for students and teachers”
and : -
“…it is not felt that the tone or content of these is consistent with the Code of
Conduct for Teachers or the basic standards we expect from members of staff in the
teaching profession.”

- 4 -
In support of the complaint reference was made to earlier investigations carried out
including a report furnished by Collier Broderick, a specialist firm that provides human
resource services who were instructed by the appellant’s employer.
Relevant statutory provisions: -
10. Section 42 of the Act of 2001 sets out, in some detail, the role and function of the
investigating committee of the respondent.
Section 42(1B) provides: -
“The investigating committee may consider a complaint relating to the matter
specified at paragraph (b) of subsection (1) notwithstanding that the conduct to
which the complaint relates occurred prior to the coming into operation of this part
where that conduct –
(a) would have constituted a criminal offence at the time that conduct occurred
and
(b) is of such a nature as to reasonably give arise to a bone fide concern that
the teacher may –
(i) harm any child or vulnerable person,
(ii) cause any child or vulnerable person to be harmed,
(iii) put any child or vulnerable person at risk from harm,
(iv) attempt to harm any child or vulnerable person, or
(v) incite another person to harm any child or vulnerable person.”
11. Section 42(9) provides: -
“Following its inquiry, the investigating committee shall –
(a) where it is of the opinion that there is a prima facie case to warrant
further action being taken in relation to a complaint, make a decision to refer
the complaint, in whole or in part, to the disciplinary committee or

- 5 -
(b) where it is of the opinion that there is no prima facie case to warrant
further action being taken in relation to a complaint, make a decision to refuse
to refer the complaint to the disciplinary committee.”
Section 42(1B) makes provision for the investigation of a complaint concerning conduct
which occurred prior to the coming into operation of Part V of the Act of 2001. Part V
makes provision for the investigation and determination of complaints made of
professional misconduct against registered teachers. Part V was only commenced on 25
July 2016.
Investigation of complaint: -
12. By letter dated 21 May 2021 the appellant was informed by a case officer from the
professional standards section, an employee of the respondent, that at a meeting of the
Executive Committee held 26 April 2021 the Executive Committee had considered the
matters referred to in the letter of 14 January 2020. Arising from that meeting the
complaint had been referred to the Investigating Committee of the respondent.
13. The contents of the letter of 21 May 2021 are of importance in considering the
various issues raised in these proceedings. I will set out the relevant passages. The letter
states: -
“The Investigating Committee
The Investigating Committee is the Committee of the Teaching Council that looks
into and gives initial consideration to complaints about registered teachers ..”
The letter enclosed for the attention of the appellant various documentation in support of
the complaint.
The letter continues: -
“Initial consideration by the Investigating Committee

- 6 -
The complaint will be considered by the Investigating Committee at its next available
meeting. Generally speaking, there are two parts to the initial consideration of a
complaint (referred to as parts 1 and 2 below) before the Committee considers
whether it will investigate the complaint.
Part 1 – when did the events occur?
If the conduct complained of took place before the commencement of Part 5 of the
Teaching Councils Acts 2001 – 2015 which was 25 July 2016, the Investigating
Committee will first have to decide if it is entitled to consider the complaint. It is
only in exceptional circumstances that the Committee can decide to proceed to
consider such a complaint. The Committee is only entitled to proceed where it
decides that: -
(a) the conduct complained of is conduct that would, if proven, have
constituted a criminal offence at the time it occurred; and
(b) the conduct is of such a nature as to reasonably give rise to a bone fide
concern that a child or a vulnerable person may be physically, sexually or
emotionally exploited or abused..”
and: -
“The complaint and inquiry process
If the Investigating Committee proceeds to investigate the complaint, you will
be given copies of any documentation received in relation to the complaint and
invited to provide submissions and information to the Investigating Committee before
it decides whether to refer the complaint to the Disciplinary Committee for an
inquiry”
and: -
“Information/submissions from you

- 7 -
You are not obliged to furnish any submissions or information in relation to the
complaint at this time. However, if you wish to do so, it would be expected that, at
this point, any submissions from you would be in relation to the two parts of the
initial consideration as described in the section of this letter entitled Initial
consideration by the Investigating Committee.”
14. Where the complaint relates to matters that occurred before 25 July 2016 the
Investigating Committee has to take a decision whether it has jurisdiction to investigate the
complaint. If the Committee so decides, it will then proceed to an inquiry as to whether a
prima facie case has been made out to warrant further action being taken by referring the
complaint (or part of the complaint), to the Disciplinary Committee of the respondent. The
terms of the letter of 21 May 2021 reflect the relevant provisions of s. 42 of the Act of
2001.
15. The appellant made written submissions, dated 18 June 2021 that ran to some fifty-
five pages. In the course of this lengthy submission he did not directly address the matters
referred to in s. 42(1B) of the Act.
Decision of 29 June 2021: -
16. The Investigating Committee met on the 29 June 2021 as per the letter of 21 May
2021. The appellant was informed of the decision taken by letter dated 6 July 2021. The
letter stated: -
“The Committee considered all of the documentation before it and decided to
proceed to consider and investigate the complaint.”
“The Committee considered the twelve social media posts and decided that ten of
those posts do amount to conduct that would have constituted a criminal offence at
the time they occurred. In this regard, the Committee noted that the corresponding
offence is section 2 of the Prohibition of Incitement to Hatred Act 1989. ---- The

- 8 -
Committee also decided that the conduct is of such a nature as to reasonably give
rise to a bone fide concern that the teacher may harm, cause harm et cetera to a
child or vulnerable person as referred to in section 42(1B) of the Teaching Council
Acts, 2001 to 2015.”
The letter informed the appellant that it would now hold an investigation into the complaint
and invited the appellant to make any further submissions if he so wished.
17. By email dated 27 July 2021 the appellant provided a detailed response in respect of
the social media posts, the subject of the complaint. This response ran to some fifty-one
pages.
18. On 21 December 2021 the appellant received a “case report” concerning the
complaint from the respondent. In response the appellant made further submissions.
19. Decision of 24 January 2022: -
By letter dated 1 February 2022 the appellant was informed that the Investigating
Committee had met on 24 January 2022 and that the complaint which they were
investigating had been referred to the Disciplinary Committee of the respondent.
The letter noted that the Investigating Committee had previously decided that two of
the twelve social media post did not amount to conduct that would have constituted a
criminal offence. The letter stated: -
‘Pursuant to section 42(9)(a) of the Teaching Council Acts 2001 to 2015 (the
“Act” the Committee formed the opinion that there is a prima facie case to
warrant further action being taken in relation to the complaint, and decided to
refer the complaint in whole to the Disciplinary Committee for an inquiry to be
held by a panel of the Disciplinary Committee on a date to be fixed.’”
Judicial review proceedings: -

- 9 -
20. By order of the High Court (Barr J.) the appellant was granted leave to seek, inter
alia, the following reliefs: -
(1) An order of certiorari quashing the decision of the respondent of 24 January
2022 (communicated by letter of 1 February 2022) to refer the complaint made
by the Chief Executive of the City of Dublin ETB dated 14 January 2021
against the applicant to the Disciplinary Committee of the respondent.
(2) An injunction precluding the respondent taking any further steps in the
aforesaid disciplinary process commenced against the applicant.
Application to the High Court: -
21. By notice of motion, dated 8 March 2024, the appellant sought the following orders
against the respondent: -
(1) An order pursuant to O. 31 of the Rules of the Superior Court requiring the
respondent to make discovery on oath of the following categories of
documents.
(i) All documentation including briefing documents or other advice in
relation to the Committee addressing its obligations under s. 42(1B) of the Act
of 2015 and the definition of “harm” as provided to or generated by or utilised
by the Investigating Committee and its members in the exercise of their
statutory function in the complaint process involving the applicant;
(ii) all documentation -- in relation to the meaning attributed to by the
Committee to what “conduct” was being investigated by them and how that
came to be viewed as “professional conduct…”
(2) An order directing the respondent to provide full and detailed particulars as
previously sought in correspondence of the grounds contained in the statement
of grounds of opposition insofar as the said grounds involve:

- 10 -
(i) the Committee addressing its obligations under s. 42(1B) of the Act of
2015 and the definition of “harm”;
(ii) the meaning attributed by the Committee to what “conduct” was being
investigated; and
(iii) why the Investigating Committee had regard to the workplace
investigation reports authored by Collier Broderick.
(3) An order pursuant to O. 84, r. 22(5) of the Rules of the Superior Courts seeking
to strike out the statement of opposition filed on behalf of the respondent.
Judgment of the High Court: -
22. The matter came on for hearing before Bolger J. in the High Court on 8 October
2024. A written judgment was delivered on 31 December 2024 refusing the reliefs sought
in the notice of motion with subsequent order of 31 January 2025.
23. In her judgment the trial judge stated: -
‘’ 7. The Investigating Committee decided at its meeting of 29 June 2021 that it did
have jurisdiction pursuant to s. 42(1B). The single substantive relief sought by the
applicant in his judicial review proceedings, for which he has been given leave, is to
quash the Investigating Committee’s later decision of 24 January 2022 to refer the
complaint to the Disciplinary Committee. The applicant has never sought to
challenge the Investigating Committee’s earlier decision of 29 June 2021.
Therefore, if the applicant succeeds in his substantive judicial review proceedings, it
is the decision of 24 January 2022 to refer the complaint to the Disciplinary
Committee that will be quashed and any other decision including that of 29 June
2021 will stand.
8. The focus of the applicant’s interlocutory application for discovery and
particulars is on the earlier decision of 29 June 2021, rather than the decision of 24

- 11 -
January 2022. The applicant argues he is entitled to know what opinion the
Investigating Committee formed in determining it had jurisdiction pursuant to s.
42(1B), the basis for that opinion and how it was formed, given that the matter will
never be revisited, including by the Disciplinary Committee if or when the complaint
is referred to them.”
24. The trial judge further held what the appellant was seeking was similar to what was
found to be an attempt to supplement a decision which was rejected by Hyland J. in
Jackson Way Properties Limited & Anor. v. The Information Commissioner [2020] IEHC
73. The trial judge noted that in a supplemental affidavit the respondent’s entire
documentary record of the disciplinary proceedings had been put before the court including
the minutes of the Investigating Committee of 29 June 2021.
25. The trial judge rejected the submission of the appellant that the statement of
opposition was not in accordance with the Rules of the Superior Courts insofar as it was
submitted that it comprised, for the most part, of denials. The trial judge considered the
content of the statement of opposition including: -
“50. The statement of opposition runs to some nine pages and twenty three
paragraphs which identify the respondent’s position on the legal issues the applicant
has set out in his pleadings, primarily the correct statutory interpretation of s. 42 of
the Act and the respondent’s compliance or non-compliance with it. It does contain
denials of the applicant’s allegations (as might be expected) but it is not limited to
bare denials as it also contains extensive pleas around what the respondent claims
was done lawfully within its jurisdiction and in compliance with the Act. …”
26. The trial judge did grant leave to the appellant to amend the statement of grounds by
the addition of three paragraphs which were directed towards the decision of 29 June 2021.
Notice of appeal: -

- 12 -
27. In his notice of appeal the appellant set out some eight grounds of appeal. In
particular, I refer to the following grounds: -
“(i) The learned trial judge erred in law and in her understanding of the nature of
the applicant’s challenge in her finding that the applicant has never sought to
challenge the decision of the Investigating Committee of the 29th June 2021. At
all material times it has been contended that what was in law to be regarded as
a preliminary view adopted by the Investigating Committee on 29th June 2021
was subsumed into the decision of 24 January 2022 to refer the complaint
against the applicant to the Disciplinary Committee of the respondent.
(iii) The learned trial judge in consequence erred in law in her understanding of
the statutory framework in the issue.
(vii) The learned trial judge erred in law in holding that the respondent should not
be required to make discovery or furnish further details whether by way of
particulars or affidavit concerning the meaning that was attributed by the
Investigating Committee to the word ‘harm’ on the basis that this had a clear
statutory meaning which it can be assumed in the absence of evidence to the
contrary the Committee was aware. …”
Consideration of appeal: -
28. The appellant did not challenge the decision of 29 June 2021. This much is clear
from the statement of grounds. The appellant submitted to this court that the decision of
29 June 2021 was a “gateway” or “preliminary” decision and thus not judicially
reviewable. It was also submitted that the said decision was analogous to a decision under
planning legislation involving substitute consent. However, this submission was not
elaborated on.

- 13 -
29. In my view, this submission of the appellant is not supported by the relevant
statutory provisions. Section 42(1B) clearly provides for a decision to be taken as to
whether the Investigating Committee has jurisdiction to consider a complaint that relates to
events that occurred prior to 25 July 2016. For such a complaint to be considered it must
meet the requirements that are clearly set out in the subsection. If it does not do so, then
that concludes the consideration of the complaint. If the complaint does meet the
requirements then there is a further inquiry under s. 42(9) to decide if there is “a prima
facie case to warrant further action being taken in relation to the complaint ..”.
30. This interpretation is reflected in the correspondence that was sent by the respondent
to the appellant referred to at para. 13 above. The letter of 21 May 2021 refers to “two
parts to the initial consideration of a complaint” and the appellant was invited to make
submissions on the matters to be considered by the Investigating Committee under s.
42(1B). This is not a “preliminary view” as submitted by the appellant. It is a “stand
alone” decision which was not the subject of any challenge by way of judicial review.
31. The appellant was informed of the outcome of the meeting of 29 June 2021 by letter
dated 6 July 2021. This letter stated, inter alia, that twelve social media posts had been
considered and it was decided that ten of those posts did meet the criteria for an inquiry by
the Investigating Committee under s. 42(9). Thus the complaint insofar as it related to two
of the twelve social media posts was dismissed, a determination in favour of the appellant.
This was not “a preliminary view”.
32. Following the decision of 29 June 2021 the appellant received “a case report” on 21
December 2021 and was invited to make further submissions to the Inquiry under s. 42(9).
33. I am in full agreement with the trial judge that the documents sought by way of
discovery and the letters seeking further particulars relate to the decision of 29 June 2021.
As there is no challenge to that decision the appellant is not entitled to these reliefs.

- 14 -
34. There is a further issue which does not appear to have been considered by the High
Court but was the subject of submissions to this court namely the legal status of the
decisions taken by the Investigating Committee and whether, in this case, they are
amenable to judicial review.
35. The various bodies that regulate and set standards for professions all have statutory
procedures to consider and adjudicate on complaints. A common feature of these
procedures is an investigative process to identify which complaints require further
consideration and which do not. Fair procedures required by natural justice will vary
depending upon what is at stake at different stages of the decision-making process.
However, it is well-settled that a person, such as the appellant, who is the subject of a
complaint does not necessarily enjoy the full panoply of the rights of fair procedures at the
investigative stage. He or she is entitled to be informed of the details of the complaint and
afforded an opportunity to make submissions. The outcome of the investigative process in
this case is no more than a finding “that there is a prima facie case to warrant further
action being taken ..”. I refer to the following passage in Law Society v. Walker [2007] 3
IR 581 where Finnegan P. described the investigative process concerning a complaint
made against a solicitor: -
“In the light of the authorities which I mention I am satisfied that the function of the
Tribunal is to consider all matters on Affidavit before it. While at this stage of the
procedures the Tribunal is not the fact finding body it may for the purposes of
deciding on whether a prima facie case is disclosed make findings of fact where the
facts are clear for example where the complaint is based on a clear misapprehension
as to the facts or the law. Subject to this the Tribunal should consider all the material
before it and determine whether the application has any real prospect of being
established at an inquiry any doubt being resolved in favour of an inquiry being held.

- 15 -
The purpose of this stage of the regulatory process is to enable complaints which are
frivolous, vexatious, misconceived or lacking in substance to be summarily disposed
of.”
36. The decisions of 29 June 2021 and 24 January 2022 are not findings that the
complaint against the appellant has been substantiated but rather that there is a case to
answer. The complaint has been referred to the Disciplinary Committee where the
appellant will enjoy full rights to fair procedures. In the course of hearing the appeal it was
confirmed that at the hearing before the Disciplinary Committee the appellant is not
constrained in the issues he may raise by reason of the decisions taken by the Investigating
Committee. The appellant is fully entitled to contest the matters considered by the
Investigating Committee on 29 June 2021 including the matters listed at s. 42(1B)(a) and
(b). The decisions of the Investigating Committee does not limit or curtail his defence to
the complaint.
37. I am satisfied that the appellant’s criticism of the statement of opposition is
misplaced. The reliefs sought in the statement of grounds are directed towards the decision
of 26 January 2022. The statement of opposition sets out clearly that this decision did not
determine the complaint but rather was part of an investigative process. Further, the
statement of opposition sets out in detail the content of correspondence that passed
between the appellant and the respondent. I am satisfied that the appellant has identified
no lack of candour on the part of the respondent. The statement of opposition does not
consist merely of denials.
38. By reason of the foregoing the appeal will be dismissed. As for costs, the provisional
view of the court is that as the respondent has been “entirely successful”, in resisting the
appeal, it is entitled to its costs. Should the appellant wish to take issue with this he may
do so by way of written submissions (not more than 1,500 words) to be delivered within 14

- 16 -
days of the date of the delivery of this judgment. The respondent may reply to this
submission, also by way of written submission (not more than 1,500 words) within a
further 14 days.
39. As this decision is being delivered electronically Binchy and Butler JJ. have
authorised me to record their agreement with it.
